So we're going to have to generate basically 40,000 menus.
Right. For your friends.
And we have a very small downtime. You know, when the stores close, we do them based on time zone,
but they need to get up by a certain time. So luckily we have the scalability of AWS behind us.
So what we do is we just queue those up. We have an SQS queue, triggers something called the menu exporter
Lambda, basically processes, takes the data from this database, spins it up how it needs to be,
creates these complex JSON menus. They all have these big object menus and the menus are very big.
So you're looking at a hundred gigs of just raw menu data that's passing through the wire to these
delivery aggregators. So luckily we scale a lot of tacos. It scales very quickly and it's able to
process that. So what they'll do is they'll generate the menus, send it to the delivery aggregators.
They'll process it when they're, when they're ready right away, or maybe a few seconds later,
and then they'll send webhooks back, basically telling us if it succeeded or failed.
We'll pipe that back in and we'll mark it in an audit table that it was processed successfully.
So that means that menu is now live on the restaurant.
So it's, it's in production and I can pull out my phone and place an order.
You can place whatever you want. It's ready to go.
Neat. So we're looking at Aurora here as a centerpiece of this architecture.
Tell me a little bit about why you're using Aurora and what benefits you're getting from that.
Mm-hmm. So Aurora was perfect for us because a lot of us had a relational background,
so it was comfortable for us. But they also handled stuff we didn't care about,
like the database administrative tasks, the scaling. Read well because they're just available.
They can auto scale for you. So on days when we have to do that, that's very valuable.
There's going to be hotspots where things are going fast. So Aurora just scales perfectly for us.
And it's able to handle the massive number of lambdas that we're queuing up.
So it's a really interesting architecture, Ravi. And I love having Aurora as that centerpiece.
It's all serverless. Any other benefits that you're getting from this architecture?
